main_summary.summary: Rosie Head, interviewed by Dr. John Dittmer in Tchula, Mississippi on March 13, 2013, recounts her work as a grassroots organizer in the Holmes County Mississippi Movement. Born in Holmes County and raised on plantations in Lexington and Greenwood, she describes sharecropping arithmetic that kept her family perpetually 'in the hole' at year-end settlement, the company store at $35 monthly for twelve people, and her father's confrontation with a plantation owner over his paycheck that forced the family to flee to Tchula. Entering the movement in 1963-64 through meetings in Greenwood with SNCC organizers, she names John Ball, Sam Block, Stokely Carmichael, Bob Moses, Lawrence Guyot, Willie Wazir Peacock, and Hollis Watkins as her early teachers. She canvassed for the 1963 Freedom Vote across Holmes County, worked closely with Hartman Turnbow, and managed the Mileston Community Center (built by Abe Osheroff) where she ran day-to-day operations, a kindergarten, and coordinated medical services through the Medical Committee for Human Rights. She organized voter mobilization that elected Robert Clark to the Mississippi state legislature in 1967, the first African American legislator since Reconstruction, and worked extensively in the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) Head Start program with Sue and Henry Lorenzi, Polly Greenberg, and Tom Levin.
key_themes: Sharecropping economics and Christmas-eve settlement fraud
SNCC voter registration in Holmes County
Mileston Community Center operations and Medical Committee for Human Rights clinic
Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) Head Start program
main_summary.historical_significance:
Head's testimony provides a Holmes County interior view of the Mississippi Freedom Movement from the vantage of a center director, not a SNCC field secretary or a celebrity organizer. Her account of the Mileston Community Center as an integrated hub of voter education, kindergarten, women's sewing, and federally-funded medical care documents the institutional pivot from outside-organized voter registration to local infrastructure that long outlasted the movement's national attention. Her firsthand description of the bank in Tchula refusing to cash CDGM payroll checks, of Mississippi Senator John Stennis blocking CDGM refunding as a 'civil rights organization,' and of the Greyhound-bus delegation of Head Start children sent to lobby Washington places Holmes County at the center of the federal-policy fight over War on Poverty funding in Mississippi.

Her naming of specific Holmes County figures (Hartman Turnbow, Bernice Montgomery, Josephine Disparti, Sam House) alongside national figures (Bob Moses, Stokely Carmichael) preserves the local-leadership substrate that the better-known histories of Mississippi Freedom Summer often elide.

chapters.title: Holmes County Childhood and Sharecropping
chapters.summary: Head was born in Holmes County to a carpenter-sharecropper father and a mother who cooked for the plantation owner. She describes growing up in Lexington and Greenwood, walking five miles to a one-room church-school for all eight grades, and the family's truck patches, cows, horses, mules, chickens, and hogs.
related_events: Mississippi rural Black education in the 1940s

chapters.title: Segregation and Christmas Settlement
chapters.summary: Head describes early awareness of segregation through her mother's work at the plantation owner's house, where she could play outside with the white child Corinne but not enter. She recounts the annual Christmas-eve plantation settlement where her father was always told he had 'come out in the hole,' the company store at $35 monthly for twelve people, and her mother making dolls from flour and meal sacks.

chapters.title: Emmett Till and Leaving the Plantation
chapters.summary: Head describes hearing about Emmett Till's murder on the radio at the store of Carolyn and Roy Bryant, where she went to school nearby. She recounts her father's confrontation with the plantation owner who tried to coerce Black women into the office to collect paychecks, and the family's overnight escape to Tchula after the boss seized his car keys.

chapters.title: Movement Entry through Greenwood SNCC Meetings
chapters.summary: Beginning in 1963-64, Head began attending civil rights meetings in Greenwood where she met SNCC organizers including John Ball, Sam Block, Stokely Carmichael, Bob Moses, Lawrence Guyot, Willie Wazir Peacock, and Hollis Watkins. The meetings addressed segregated public accommodations, second-class citizenship, taxation without representation, and voter registration as the source of political power.

chapters.title: Hartman Turnbow and Holmes County Leadership
chapters.summary: Head describes Hartman Turnbow as fearless and willing to draw his pistol at meetings to demonstrate self-defense, contrasting his style with quieter local leaders. She recalls Turnbow visiting her home in later years and predicting the rise of mass incarceration of young Black men.

chapters.title: 1963 Freedom Vote Canvassing
chapters.summary: Head canvassed Holmes County for the fall 1963 Freedom Vote with Aaron Henry and Ed King as the mock-election candidates, working through polling places in Thornton and Mileston. She describes white farmers cursing canvassers off their property, the climate of intimidation that left some Black residents fearful of being evicted from plantations, and Northern donors sending food and clothing to support displaced families.

chapters.title: Ku Klux Klan Intimidation at the Holmes County Center
chapters.summary: Head recounts working nights at the Holmes County Community Center with a small staff including a worker named Frian from California, and being shot at by Klan trucks circling the building. She describes the firebombs ('cocktails') thrown at activist houses, and adults taking turns sitting up at night to watch the family home.

chapters.title: Freedom Summer 1964 in Holmes County
chapters.summary: Head describes the 1964 Freedom Summer in Holmes County, where about 30 volunteers worked on voter registration, freedom schools, and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party. She recounts marching from Mississippi Valley State to Jackson during a period of Movement-related murder, with white spectators throwing rocks from boxcars while highway patrol watched.

chapters.title: Building the Mileston Community Center
chapters.summary: Abe Osheroff came from California to build the Mileston Community Center; Head's father helped build it. After construction, the gas company refused service and the telephone line was denied, requiring wood heaters. Highway patrol parked at the railroad crossing to ticket and intimidate visitors. The Medical Committee for Human Rights — including Josephine Disparti, Helen Richardson, and Dr. Albert Vorspan — established a clinic in a small room they built onto the Center.

chapters.title: Managing the Mileston Center and Pre-Head Start Kindergarten
chapters.summary: When Henry and Sue Lorenzi prepared to leave Mileston, they asked Head to manage the Community Center. She handled records, newsletters, county-wide communications, and supervised four young women on staff. Before federal Head Start funding arrived, the Center ran a kindergarten using a truck donated by Abe Osheroff to collect children from across the county, with sewing machines for making children's clothes.

chapters.title: Voter Registration and the Voting Rights Act
chapters.summary: Head recounts being locked in a small registration room at the courthouse with a guard dog at the door for four hours after completing the literacy test. She describes the chancery clerk lecturing her about how her grandparents would never have come to vote. Following the 1965 Voting Rights Act, federal registrars arrived and the family learned they had passed the literacy test a year earlier but had never been notified.

chapters.title: Mobilizing the Vote: 1966 and Bernice Montgomery
chapters.summary: Head describes the 1966 campaign for the congressional race where Mr. Hayes was running. She organized car pools, made the Center a calling hub, and coordinated countywide pickups. She notes that Black professionals — teachers and preachers — were largely absent from the early movement, fearing job loss, with the schoolteacher Bernice Montgomery as a notable exception who attended meetings from the beginning.

chapters.title: Robert Clark's 1967 Legislative Election
chapters.summary: Head describes serving as receiving-and-returning manager at the polls in 1967 when Robert Clark won election to the Mississippi state legislature — the first African American legislator in the twentieth century. She recalls a drunk white truck driver coming to remove his wife from polling duties, and the routine intimidation of rough-looking white men parking themselves at the door to scare away Black voters.
related_events: Robert Clark Mississippi state legislature election 1967

chapters.title: CDGM and Tchula Bank Refusal
chapters.summary: Head describes the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) Head Start program brought by Sue and Henry Lorenzi, with training in Mound Bayou and program management with Polly Greenberg and Tom Levin. The Tchula bank initially refused to cash CDGM payroll checks; a supermarket owner finally intervened. She recounts overhearing the Lexington merchant Cohen and others discussing how the program would 'hurt' Black residents who weren't 'used to suffering.'
related_events: Child Development Group of Mississippi founding 1965

chapters.title: Head Start Pedagogy and the Greyhound to Washington
chapters.summary: Head describes the Mileston Head Start program: bathing children in the Center's shower (many had never used indoor plumbing), teaching them to write their names, color recognition, and basic literacy through experiential outdoor counting exercises. When Senator John Stennis blocked CDGM refunding, the program continued unfunded for three months; Head's children rode a chartered Greyhound bus to Washington DC with teacher Zelma to lobby congressmen, leading to renewed funding by May.

chapters.title: College, CDA Credential, and Continuing Work
chapters.summary: Head describes her education: a two-year degree in 1978, a Child Development Associate (CDA) credential, and subsequent college coursework. She describes her diabetes diagnosis interrupting her studies, and continued Head Start work seven years to the date of the interview, with Dr. McLean advising her on degree-completion at a Delta college.
